title: Share of children achieving minimum reading proficiency by the end of lower-secondary age
caption:
Share of children achieving minimum reading proficiency by the end of lower-secondary age, 2022

Percentage of children at lower secondary school age meeting reading comprehension standards. This data accounts for school completion rates to estimate reading skills for all children, not just those in school.

The bar chart ranks countries by the percentage of children at lower secondary school age who meet reading comprehension standards, with the metric adjusted to account for school completion rates so that estimates cover all children rather than only those currently enrolled. The vertical axis measures this share from 0% to 80%, while the horizontal axis lists individual nations ordered from highest proficiency on the left to lowest on the right. A color-coded legend categorizes these nations into four income groups: low-income countries in dark purple, lower-middle-income countries in light purple, upper-middle-income countries in light green, and high-income countries in dark green. Ireland leads the distribution with a proficiency share exceeding 80%, followed closely by Australia and Lithuania, all of which fall within the high-income category. Spain and Turkey also appear near the top of the ranking, with Turkey representing the upper-middle-income group. As the bars descend in height, the composition shifts; Greece and Moldova appear in the middle of the range with values between 60% and 70%, while Brazil and Kazakhstan sit lower with shares around 40%. The lowest values on the chart belong to Azerbaijan, the Dominican Republic, and Cambodia, where the share of proficient children drops below 20%. Cambodia records the minimum value visible, falling well under the 10% mark. The data shows a clear gradient where high-income countries predominantly occupy the upper portion of the chart, while lower-middle-income and low-income countries cluster at the bottom, with upper-middle-income nations distributed across the middle and lower sections.
